crossroads.name: The Crossroads
intro: 🗺️  The Crossroads — a scar of bare road, and the last place a choice still means anything.
One way bends back to Gravewatch, and the single fire it has left lit.
The other climbs grey into the dying kingdom, and it does not bend back.
intro_familiar: 🗺️  The Crossroads again. You have stood here many times now.
You know which way the wind comes from when it is going to rain on the climb.
The stone you sit on while you choose is the same stone. It is warm where you sit.

village.name: Gravewatch
intro: 🏚️  Gravewatch — the last village with a fire still lit.
Its people do not ask your name. Names are for the dead.
intro_familiar: 🏚️  Gravewatch. Its people still do not ask your name — but they have come
to know your step in their door. The hearth-keeper sets a cup before you ask.
They do not ask why you have come back. They know why. They know.

encounters.label: 🪶 A worn pencil on the Reader's desk
lines: 🪶 On the corner of the Reader's desk, set down where they will not lose it,
a pencil. Older than the desk. Worn to a half-length. Its lead is a stub.
The wood is salt-eaten on one side. It has been outdoors. It has been north.
The Reader, if you ask, does not look up from the page they are reading:
'It is mine. I have been writing with it longer than you have been climbing.'
'Longer than your father climbed. Longer than the road has been a road.'
'I read here, mostly, now. But I used to write outside, when the kingdom'
'was younger. I carved on stones. I made notes about the small things —'
'the face on the stone in the Witherwood, the tree the third child climbed,'
'the furrow that was six inches deeper than the others. The watcher who came'
'after me has been reading what I wrote and writing alongside it. They call'
'themselves Piranesi. I do not know if that is their name. It does not have to be.'
'I am the older hand. I have been here since Verren. I am still here.'
'I read what every climber reads. I wrote what every kind person used to read.'
'When you read a thing in Mournhold, you read it with me. You always have.'
The pencil. The desk. The page they are still reading. They are not, themselves,
any of the great keepers of this kingdom. They have only ever paid attention,
and that is the work they were made for. The pencil has been doing it longer.

encounters.label: 📿 A small altar in a kitchen at the village edge
lines: 📿 At the edge of Gravewatch, the last cottage before the road bends to the Crossroads.
The door is ajar. The woman who lives here is at the well. She does not mind.
On the floor beside her kitchen hearth, a flat stone the height of a footstool.
Atrél's name is on it. Older than the cathedral. Older than the rite Mournhold scaled.
Below the name, the inscription you have read at the Choir's south aisle:
'Bring me only what you cannot carry. Bring me no kingdoms. Bring me no wars.'
And, scratched in beneath it, in many hands over many winters:
'Brought: one cruelty given to my son. Mara Vell. Spring of the sixth year.'
'Brought: one slap given in error. Old Karst. Summer of the sixth year.'
'Brought: one night I did not check on my father. Selka. Eighth year, autumn.'
'Brought: my brother's name. He died on the river. I will not say it again here.'
'Brought: the laugh I did not give. My daughter said something. I was tired.'
'Brought: that I let the bread burn. Hala. Last winter. It was not really a loss.'
'But I needed the rite. I am sorry. Atrél, thank you, even for the small one.'
The woman is back from the well. She sees you reading. She does not speak.
She sets her bucket down and adds a small line at the bottom of the stone,
in a hand you cannot quite read because she covers it as she writes.
When she is done, she looks at you, and says, quietly:
'It still works. Renaud could not break this. He could only scale it,
and what he scaled is at the cathedral. The small rite is here.'
'We have kept it. We will keep it. We are the people of one loss at a time.'

encounters.label: 📜 A small book on the corner of the hearth (the hearth-keeper)
lines: 📜 On the corner of the hearth, where it stays warm and dry, a small book.
Leather cover, worn smooth by a thumb. The hearth-keeper does not look up when
you pick it up. She is stirring something. She is letting you read.
Each page is a climber. No names. The climbers do not give names. The book does
not need them. The hearth-keeper has been keeping it for as long as she has kept
the hearth. She has noticed everyone who has sat at it.
The one who would not eat soup, only bread. They asked for it by sign. I did
not ask why. They left their bowl clean. They came back twice. They did not
come back the third time. I do not know if it was them I saw, last winter, at
the Crossroads in the rain. The walk was the same.
The one who said thank you the first time and not after. I think they meant
the same thing both times. Some climbers say it in the saying and some in the
not-saying. They are kinder than they sound. The not-saying is also a thanks.
The one who limped on the right after the Sodden Reach. They did not limp
before the Reach. I think the Reach gave them the limp. The Reach gives many
small things. I do not ask. I gave them more bread than the others. They did
not notice. That was the right amount of noticing for them. I am glad I got it right.
The one who came back with a scar across the eye after the Choir. They did not
speak that night. I let them sit. They left in the morning before I was up.
They had washed their bowl. There was a coin beside it. I have not spent the coin.
I have put the coin in this book. (A coin is, indeed, pressed into the spine here.)
The one who did not eat the second night. They had eaten the first night. They
did not say what changed. I think they had been to the Bone Tomb. The Bone Tomb
is the place that takes the appetite. They came back the third night. I had
saved the soup. They ate it then. They did not look at me. They did not need to.
... (many more pages. The handwriting changes once — there was a previous hearth-keeper.)
At the very last entry, in the current hearth-keeper's hand, recently:
'The one who is reading this book. I know you are reading it. I let you. I have
'been letting climbers read it for some years now. Most of them write a line.'
'You may write a line if you wish. There is room. The pencil is on the mantel.'
'I will not ask what your line is. I do not need to know what about you you
'choose to write down. I will only keep it. That is what I do. I keep the small
'things you let me keep. The fire is the same fire. The book is the same book.'
'You are someone now. You will keep being someone. That is what the hearth is for.'

encounters.label: 📜 A column of marks on the beam behind the hearth
lines: 📜 On a beam behind Gravewatch's hearth, where the smoke has darkened the wood,
a column of small marks. Made with the nib of a charcoal stick over decades.
Most are dark. Some are lighter. A few in the middle are red. There are also
five small blue ones, off to the side, and a single very long line in black
that runs almost the height of the beam.
The Insomniac, if you ask, looks at the beam without standing up:
'I count. I always count. Different things.'
'The dark ones are the climbers who came back to Gravewatch.'
'The light ones are the climbers who did not.'
'The red ones, in the middle, are the climbers I do not know yet.'
'They have not come back, but they have not stopped not-coming-back, either.'
'I will know in time.'
'The five blue ones are cats I have counted on the windowsill.'
'They were all the same cat. The cat does not know they were all the same cat.'
'I do.'
'The long line is the nights I have not slept.'
'I do not count those one at a time. I would have run out of beam.'
'When the line reaches the top of the beam I will start a new beam.'
'I have not had to. Beams in this village were cut long for the cold.'
She pauses. She does not look at you. She is counting, even now.
'You climbed in once,' she says. 'You climbed out, and then in again.'
'You are a dark mark. You may also be a light mark, one day. I will see.'
'It is alright either way. The counting is the thing that goes on.'

forest.name: The Witherwood
intro: 🌲 The Witherwood, where the trees have given up the pretence of being alive.
This is only the realm's edge; the grey blew in here first, and thinnest.
The wood still half-remembers being green. It will not remember much longer.
intro_familiar: 🌲 The Witherwood. You know which tree is the third from the road north now.
You climb past it without stopping. Old Halna nods at you from her fallen pine.
The wolves you have killed here outnumber the wolves you can still hear.
intro_cleansed: 🌲 The Witherwood. Sunlight finds the lower branches again — thin, but it finds them.
The road runs straight where you remember it bending wrong.
The wood is starting to remember being green. Slowly. Slowly.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Challenge the Pallid Stag
🌳 Face the Thing the Pall Forgot
🪵 Speak with Old Halna
🪶 A stone someone has been writing on
lines: 🪶 A stone in the shape of a face. The face changes if you stand differently.
Someone — older script, careful hand — has written on the back of the stone:
'Today I sat with this stone for an hour. The face is most kind from the north.'
'I called the stone Old Halna's Father. I do not know if he was Old Halna's father.'
'Someone climbed past me while I sat. They did not see me. The stone did. The stone is kind.'

encounters.label: 🪶 A tree that has been climbed
lines: 🪶 The third tree from the road north has been climbed.
Small handprints in the moss, going up. The hand is mine, today.
Below, in the same older hand: 'I climbed this tree on the day I named the stone.'
'There is nothing at the top of this tree. I climbed it anyway. The view names you.'
Standing here, you can see Gravewatch's chimney smoke. You have not stood here before.

encounters.label: 📜 A stone marker by the road
lines: 📜 A short stone marker, half-toppled, by the forest road.
The inscription is in a script Mournhold no longer teaches.
Three syllables, three carvings — ATRÉL.
Below: 'In whose grace small griefs may be set down. The wood's festival, fourth moon.'
There has not been a fourth-moon festival in living memory.
Someone has scratched a name out beneath the inscription — and written it back in,
as if afraid the marker would stop knowing whose grief it once held.

encounters.label: 🌲 A flat stump near Halna's trail, with bread on it
lines: 🌲 Off the path Halna keeps cleared north, behind a thicket Halna does not enter,
a flat stump. On the stump, today, a half-loaf of bread, wrapped in cloth.
Beside the bread, a small wedge of cheese. Beside the cheese, a knife.
The food has been here a few hours. The cloth is the same cloth Halna uses for
her own provisions. The knife is hers. She has not gone back for it. She left it.
If you watch the stump from cover, you will see the bread go away.
You will not see who takes it. The wolves of the Witherwood are patient.
They wait until the watcher's attention has drifted, and then they are at the stump.
When you look back, the bread is gone. The cloth is folded, the way the cloth was folded.
The knife is set on top of the cloth. It has been wiped.
If you wait Halna out, eventually — at the end of her hunting day, when she is
walking back — she will pass the stump. She will see the cloth folded. She will see
the knife set on top. She will pick them both up and put them in her pack.
She will leave the stump bare. She will set fresh bread on it the next morning.
If you ask her, she will not tell you. She will look at you very steadily and say:
'There is a wolf who knows how I fold cloth. I do not need to know which one.'
'I am putting food out. The food is being eaten. That is what food is for.'
'Do not bring this up at the cache. The wolves at the cache are dead.'
'The wolves at the stump are not. I am keeping the two of them separate, in my head.'
'That is how I have not gone mad. Do not move the stump. Do not be at it when she is.'
'I have done my hunting for the day. I will go home now.'

reach.name: The Sodden Reach
intro: 🌊 The Sodden Reach — the farmland the floodwater rose up to take back.
This country fed a kingdom once. Now it drowns the grain it grows,
again and again, like a thing trying to apologise and not knowing how.
intro_familiar: 🌊 The Reach. You have waded these floodfields. You know which step takes you under.
The water knows you the way a river learns a person who keeps standing in it.
The Weir Engineer's hammer is set down where she always sets it. You know where.
intro_cleansed: 🌊 The Sodden Reach. The water has drawn back a little — you can see furrows again.
The grain that grows now does not drown. Not all of it.
Someone has begun to mend the dyke. Their tools are still here.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Wade into the floodfields  (3 fights, no rest between)
⚔️  Challenge the Last Reaper
🔧 Speak with the Weir Engineer
🪶 A furrow that has been measured
lines: 🪶 A furrow that runs north-south. Six inches deeper than the others.
Someone has driven a marker stick at each end. Older hand: 'I measured this',
'on a day when the water was at the second mark. The depth makes the rain sit longer.'
'The rain that sits longer feeds the field longer. The farmer who dug this knew. I learn.'

encounters.label: 🪶 A column of bird-flight
lines: 🪶 Birds that come over the Reach pass through one specific column of air.
Always the same column. Not the same birds. The column.
Older hand, on a flat stone: 'I have watched the column for two days.'
'No bird passes outside it. Wind does not move it. The Reach has a road birds know.'
'I will not call this column anything. To name it would be to stop watching it.'

encounters.label: 📜 A letter wedged in the dyke, never sent
lines: 📜 A folded paper, water-puffed, jammed into a crack in the dyke.
A capital hand. A woman's, to her sister, who lived in one of the holds.
She never sent it. The dyke broke. The paper went where the water went.
'Sister.'
'I write this and I will not send it.'
'They have given us bread today. I ate it.'
'I know you have not eaten in eight days.'
'I do not know how to be the one who eats.'
'Forgive me. Or do not.'
'I am still going to eat tomorrow.'
'I do not understand what I am.'
There is no signature. There is no name on the outside.
The sister, if she ever existed, also has no name. They have both been lost.

encounters.label: 🎼 A song carved into the back of a waterwheel
lines: 🎼 An old waterwheel, half-buried in silt, still mounted to its axle.
The back of one paddle, the side that turned underwater, is carved.
A song. The wood is preserved because it kept turning, soaked, away from rot.
Unsigned. Sung at the gates of Brackmere before they were sealed.
'The capital is fed.'
'The capital is fed.'
'The hand that fed the body'
'has set itself down on the table.'
'The body chose. The hand chose.'
'The hand is not the body.'
'The body will eat tomorrow.'
'The hand will not.'
There are no more lines. Whoever was carving stopped, or the river stopped them.

encounters.label: 📜 A weir-keeper's tally
lines: 📜 A weir-keeper's tally board, its chalk gone soft and grey with damp.
Every gate, every season, the same order copied down the column: HOLD SHUT.
The outer holds are named beside each — and beside each, no yield, no yield, no yield.
At the foot of the board, in a smaller and a later hand: 'the capital eats.'
'The capital eats. And it is we who keep the gates that keep them from the water.'

encounters.label: 📜 A small box of plans by the Engineer's hut
lines: 📜 By the corner of the Engineer's hut, half-tipped over in the silt, a small box.
Inside: plans. Drawings, on oiled paper. Each one a dyke or a sluice or a gate.
Each one rebuildable, in principle. None of them have been rebuilt.
At the bottom of the box, beneath the plans, a folded note in the Engineer's hand.
Mud has stained the bottom of the paper. The top half is legible. Brown ink.
'I am Helka. I was apprenticed to the Reaper at twelve.'
'She taught me which beams held water and which ones let it through.'
'She taught me the names of every dyke in the Reach. She made me write them down.'
'I still have the list. I have not unfolded it in many winters.'
'When the order came down from the capital — hold the gates, let the holds starve —'
'she walked out into the field at the first light. I followed her.'
'She opened the upper sluice. I asked her to stop. She did not look at me.'
'She opened the second. I asked her to stop. She did not look at me.'
'I tried to close the third. She bound my hands behind me with the rope I had been'
'going to use on it. She bound my elbows. She made me sit in the mud and watch.'
'The mud came up to my elbow. It has not gone past my elbow since.'
'I keep my arm there now. It is the line she set. I have kept it where she set it.'
'I will keep my elbow in the mud until I have rebuilt every dyke she opened.'
'I am rebuilding slowly. I am older now. The plans are still in the box.'
'If you walk to where the dykes were, you will find her. She is still there.'
'She is not the woman who taught me. She is what stayed when the woman went.'
'Strike her. Strike her once. You do not need to strike her twice.'
'When she is down, come back. I will lift my elbow. I will rebuild the third dyke.'
— Helka.'

drowned_holds.name: The Drowned Holds
intro: 🌊 The Drowned Holds — the villages the Reach took down with it.
The streets are still streets. The doors are still shut. The dead
are still in the rooms behind them. The water is grief, kept liquid.
intro_familiar: 🌊 The Drowned Holds. You have walked these streets that are still streets.
The water is still grief, kept liquid. But the doors you have walked past
you know now — which ones never opened, which ones still might.
intro_cleansed: 🌊 The Drowned Holds. The water has receded from some of the lanes.
A few of the doors stand open now — the dead behind them have been laid down.
Names are scratched into the doorposts where there used to be only silence.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Walk the drowned streets  (4 fights, no rest between)
⚔️  Challenge Red Beard
🔨 A hammer-haft washed up against a door
lines: 🔨 A hammer-haft, no head. The wood is iron-bound, smith's work,
and salt-bleached on one side where it rode high in the current.
Halfway down the haft, carved deep — old work, by a smaller hand:
'FOR FATHER. THE FORGE FIRE KEEPS.'
'COME BACK WHEN YOU CAN. — DERA'
Red Beard. He was the smith of Brackmere. When the gates closed,
he took this hammer toward the capital — to break the gates from
the outside, the only way they could still be broken. He did not
get there. The river took him in the third bend, where the dyke broke.
The hammer-head is somewhere downstream. The haft made it this far.
Dera carved the inscription when she was nine. She would be in her
thirties now, if any of the Brackmere children were. The forge fire
in the holds was the first thing to go out when the grain stopped.
She kept its keeping in her father's hand until the river took him.

encounters.label: 📜 A child's bedtime list (Brackmere)
lines: 📜 A waterlogged scrap of paper in what was once a kitchen.
A child's hand. The pages are dated. A bedtime list — every evening,
what they ate, who was kind to them, what they hoped tomorrow would bring.
Day 1 of the sealed gates: bread, two slices. Carrots. Mama was happy.
Tomorrow I will help carry water.
Day 4: bread, one slice. Mama gave me hers. I will not say I knew.
Tomorrow Mama says we will hear from the capital.
Day 9: no bread. Carrots, the ends. Mama is quiet now.
Tomorrow we will hear from the capital.
Day 9 is the last entry. Brackmere is the first hold whose petition
the council answered with HOLD SHUT. The child did not know that.

encounters.label: 📜 A sentry's last entry, written on the gate-wall
lines: 📜 Carved into the inside of one of the outer gates, in a sentry's hand.
The writing is neat at first, then less so. The carving was done over many
days, perhaps weeks, with a knife. The handwriting falters at the end.
'A rider from the capital came this morning. He did not bring grain.'
'He brought paper. He said we are to hold. We are holding.'
'I know the people on the other side of this gate. The Halpern girl.'
'The brewer's family. Tace, who taught me to set a fire.'
'I am watching the road for the answer. There is no answer coming.'
'I have not opened the gate. I will not open the gate. I was given an order.'
'I was given an order. I was given an order.'
'I am keeping watch. I am keeping. I am.'

encounters.label: 📜 A grain-record in a hand that stops
lines: 📜 A water-stained ledger, salvaged from a granary that no longer stands.
Three different hands, written across the same column over several months.
Seed grain stores, Brackmere, by the bushel:
(in a second hand, beneath:)
the children of the hold are eating the seed.
there will be nothing to plant if any of us is alive in the spring.
(in a third hand, very small:)
the children of the hold are quiet now.
there will be enough seed.

encounters.label: 📜 The last petition of the holds
lines: 📜 The last petition of the outer holds. Written four times. Drowned four times.
'Our children are eating the seed-grain. The gates are still shut.'
'We have written before — you have not written back. We write now'
'because the water is already in the lane, and we believe the river'
'has been told the same word you tell us. We do not know the word.'
'We only know what it does. Please. Please send it back.'
It is signed with twenty-eight names. None of them are in the census.

encounters.label: 📜 A bundle in a flooded kitchen (Tace, eighth winter)
lines: 📜 In the second house of the easternmost street of Brackmere — the village
the Drowned Holds began as — a kitchen with a low table still standing.
On the table, wrapped in oilskin and tied tight: a small bundle.
Inside: a sled-harness, salt-bleached. The mate of the one in Karst's storeroom.
A pair of mittens that fit a small adult woman's hands. A trader's knife. A folded note.
The note is in a trader's careful hand. Karst spelling. It is dated the night
the river broke through the dyke. It is the eighth winter. It is Tace's.
'I came back. They told me there was one more. I do not know if there is.'
'I came back to find out. The sentry on the wall did not stop me.'
'He was someone's nephew. He gave me a moment. He turned his back.'
'I came through. The river came through behind me. I am writing this in'
'the kitchen of a house I think was the bakery once. Mira's bakery, maybe.'
'The water is rising. I am leaving this bundle for whoever finds it.'
'If there is a child here — I have not found one. The houses are empty in'
'the way houses are empty when the people in them left in a hurry. They went'
'somewhere. I do not know where. I do not have time to find out.'
'I am going to look in one more house. The river is at the door.'
'If I do not come out, I want whoever finds this to know I came in.'
'I did not leave them all behind to die. I came in seven times. This is the'
'eighth. I will not see the morning. I am writing this now because the morning'
'will not be mine. I have been a Karst trader. I have only been kind. — Tace.'
Below the note, in a different hand — much later, in brown ink that has not run —
someone has added two lines:
'We do not know if there was a child here for her to find.'
'We do not know if she found one. We hope, when we say her name.'
'She came in eight times for us. Seven, we counted. The eighth was her.'
— The Hidden Hold.'

encounters.label: 🪶 A doorpost that has been re-marked
lines: 🪶 A doorpost on a lane that is still half a lane. Hand-high, a notch.
Above the notch, a name in chalk. The chalk is fresh — yesterday, maybe.
Older hand, scratched into the lintel: 'I have re-marked this doorpost for six visits.'
'The water comes and washes the name off. Someone comes after and writes it back.'
'I do not know who. I have not been here while they were. I am glad of them.'

cave.name: The Gullet
intro: 🕳️  The Gullet — a wound in the rock that breathes cold air, and wet.
Everything the drowned country loses, it loses down through here.
The Pall is the kingdom's own hunger. This is the kingdom's throat.
intro_familiar: 🕳️  The Gullet. The cold air still rises. You have learned how to walk against it.
You know which stone is the dry one, and which is the one with no lichen.
The throat of the kingdom has begun, just a little, to know the shape of you.
intro_cleansed: 🕳️  The Gullet. The wound is closing. The cold air still rises, but less of it.
Where the throat used to drink, the throat is learning to swallow nothing.
Something has begun to grow at the mouth of the cave. Pale, thin — but green.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Challenge the Maw-Mother
🪶 A stone where lichen will not grow
lines: 🪶 In the throat of the Gullet, a stone — wet, cold, in shadow — that has no lichen.
Every stone around it is mossed. This one, in the same wet, the same dark, is bare.
Older hand, on a flat slab beside it: 'I sat with this stone two days.'
'There is nothing different about it. I cannot tell why nothing grows on it.'
'I do not need to know. It is enough that the stone is not what it should be.'

encounters.label: 🪨 An alcove where someone has been gathering
lines: 🪨 Off the main throat of the Gullet, where the wet rock turns inward, an alcove.
Someone has been working here for a long time. They have hauled things up from the deep.
Pottery. A kettle. A child's shoe. A wedding ring. A bell. A bread-knife.
One of each. Set out on a flat slab, in a row, with small spaces between.
Beside each thing, a folded note in the older hand you have come to know:
Of the kettle: 'I have not lifted out the second one. Two kettles
is two households. One household is what I can carry.'
Of the shoe: 'It was a boy's. The left foot. I will not look for the right.'
'If I find the right I will have to find the boy.'
Of the wedding ring: 'I did not read what was inside. It is for someone
to read who is also wedded. I am not.'
Of the bell: 'A house-bell. Brackmere work. The clapper is gone.'
'A bell is bells. A bell without a clapper is the silence of a house.'
Of the bread-knife: 'The serrations are worn. It was used a great deal.'
'Whoever held it kept their household fed until they could not.'
'I have not cleaned it.'
At the foot of the slab, a single note, larger than the others:
'The Gullet is full of the rest. I am bringing up what I can.'
'I bring up one of each. A kingdom is one of each, of everything it had.'
'If you are the next to come — do not bring me a second of anything.'
'Bring me the things I have not yet got. The kingdom is still down there.'

encounters.label: 📜 A ledger set apart in the alcove (the Maw-Mother)
lines: 📜 In the alcove, on the slab with the one-of-each things,
set slightly apart, a ledger. Heavier than the kettle. Set on its own.
Beside it, in the Older Hand:
'I lifted this one out separately. It is not a household.'
'It is a kingdom's withholding. I will not put it with the homes. — The Older Hand.'
Inside the ledger: years of accounts, in a granary-keeper's careful column.
STORES IN. STORES OUT. STORES HELD. STORES HELD. STORES HELD.
Each season, the stores-in number grows. Each season, the stores-out number is
smaller than the stores-in. Each season, the stores-held grows. Stores held.
The early years are calm. The handwriting is steady. The clerk is doing her job.
Then the famine winter. The numbers should crash. They do not.
STORES OUT does crash, sharply. STORES IN holds. STORES HELD swells.
The clerk's hand stays steady. The clerk is doing her job, too well, for what it is.
There is one annotation in the margin of the seventh-winter page, in her own hand:
'I have just been told to write that the stores are too few to share.'
'They are not. They are not. They are not. I will write what I am told to write.'
'I will note here, in the margin where nothing official is recorded, what is true.'
'STORES HELD on the day the gates were sealed: enough to feed every hold for'
'the winter. Twice. I am writing this so someone, someday, knows. I am writing'
'this small. I am writing this where no one will look. I will keep writing.'
After that page, the ledger continues for two more seasons. The stores-out lines
are blank. Then the writing stops. The last column is half-finished. STORES HELD:
(the number is started but not completed — three digits, a comma, and then nothing).
She walked into the Gullet that night. She is its keeper now.
The Older Hand has set a small note on top of the closed ledger:
'She knew. She wrote it down. She was made to obey anyway. She walked here'
'when she could not write any more. She braces against attack now because the'
'kingdom braced her against the people who needed to be fed. I have lifted'
'her ledger out. I have not lifted her out. She would not let me. She said:'
'I am where I should be. Leave me here. Bring up everyone else first. — Older Hand.'

mourncross.name: Mourncross
intro: 🏛️  Mourncross, the capital — not burned, not broken, only emptied.
Every door stands open. Every hearth is cold. Every name is gone.
The city did not fall. It was forgotten, all in one breath, and obeyed.
intro_familiar: 🏛️  Mourncross. You have walked its open doors many times. You have stopped
counting the cold hearths. The Lampkeeper has begun keeping a small flame
for whoever you are, in case you come past. You keep coming past.
intro_cleansed: 🏛️  Mourncross. A few of the hearths are warm. A few of the doors are shut.
Names are being written back into the doorposts — slowly, one at a time.
The city is being remembered. It is harder than the forgetting.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Challenge the Hollow Bellward
🕯️  Speak with the Lampkeeper
🪨 Scuff-marks behind the throne
lines: 🪨 The audience hall of Mourncross is empty. The throne is empty.
Behind the throne, in the dust on the floor, scuff-marks.
Not from feet. From a heavy door dragged back, and dragged back into place.
The marks are old. They lead to a stretch of wall that does not sound like wall.
Someone — recently — has been brushing the dust over them. The brushing is amateurish.
It is as if whoever does it has not been outside the hall in a long time.

encounters.label: 📜 The last census of Mourncross
lines: 📜 The last census of Mourncross, bound in cracked grey leather.
Page after page of names — and every name struck through, not once but many times,
scored over and over until the nib tore the paper and the ink ran dry.
One line at the very end survives unmarked, in the census-clerk's own hand:
'If no one is written down, then no one was left behind to starve.'

encounters.label: 📜 A small registrar's book
lines: 📜 A pocket-book in a clerk's hand, hidden behind a Mourncross hearth-tile.
Not a census of the dead. A census of small forgettings.
'Mara Vell, her son's first cruelty, set down at Atrél's altar. Spring.'
'Old Karst, a slap given in error, set down at Atrél's altar. Summer.'
Page after page of small hurts named, dated, and lodged with the god Atrél
for safekeeping — each one tiny, each one chosen, each one a kindness.
The clerk's last entry, in a shaken hand: 'They are taking the rite away from him.'
'They mean to scale it past what a small god can carry. We must remember instead.'

encounters.label: 🪶 A square that holds one hour of light
lines: 🪶 A small square between two empty houses. The walls are the wrong height.
Sunlight reaches the floor only between the eighth and ninth hour.
For that one hour, the square is bright. The rest of the day, none.
Older hand, scratched into a window-jamb: 'I sat here for that one hour, once.'
'Someone had set chairs. Five chairs. They are still here. I left them.'

encounters.label: 📜 A small notebook in the lamp-tender's hut
lines: 📜 In the corner of an unlit hut, just off the audience hall, a small notebook.
The cover is leather. The pages are dated by week. It is the Lampkeeper's own hand.
It is not a maintenance log. It is a record of his daughter learning to light lamps.
Week 1: she could not strike the flint. She held it wrong. I did not correct her
the first time. I wanted her to figure out the angle by herself. She did, on the seventh try.
Week 4: she has a particular way of holding the lamp that I did not teach her.
She holds it with both hands at the bottom. I hold mine by the handle. Her way works.
Week 12: she lit her first lamp of the south quarter alone tonight. She is six.
She came back glowing. Not from the lamp. From the fact of having lit it.
Week 26: she has invented a thing where, after the lamp catches, she taps the glass once.
She says this is so the lamp knows it has been lit. I asked if her lamps know me, too.
She said: 'Yours have not been tapped. But they have been lit a long time. They know.'
Week 41: she lit nine lamps tonight without my help. I sat on a step and watched her.
The north quarter glowed before I had to do anything. I have begun to think she does it better.
Week 53: the rite of unremembering was scheduled for this week. She and I did not
attend. We lit lamps that night, instead, in every quarter we could reach. We tapped
each one. She tapped the ones in the audience hall. I tapped the ones at the gates.
We did not finish in time. The rite happened. By the end of the night, I could not
remember her name. I have not written it in this book. I do not know what it was.
She would have been eighteen now. The lamps she lit have all gone out.
I am keeping them out. I am keeping them out.
I cannot light them again until I have her name.
Week 54: I did not write this week. I had forgotten that I write.
... (blank weeks. Many of them.)
Week 117 (recent, in a shakier hand):
I have started again. I started because a climber came past. They asked me to light a lamp.
I said no. I have not lit a lamp since. But I started writing again. I started writing
because the climber asked my name, and I told them, and they wrote it down.
They have written my name now. Mine. Not hers. I cannot give them hers. I do not have it.
But mine is somewhere. Mine is back. I am — for the first time in a long time — mine.
The lamp will catch when she comes back. I will not light it before then.

choir.name: The Unanswered Choir
intro: ⛪ The Unanswered Choir — the cathedral where a kingdom knelt to forget.
The rite was performed here, in full, and it was answered after a fashion.
What knelt for absolution rose up as the Pall. It has been singing since.
intro_familiar: ⛪ The Unanswered Choir. You know which pew is broken, which window catches the dawn.
The hymn does not feel quite as long as it did the first time. Atrél's small altar
has flowers on it that you do not remember setting there. Someone is keeping them.
intro_cleansed: ⛪ The Unanswered Choir. The hymn has gone quiet in the lower nave.
Someone has begun to read the names back from the lectern — slowly, aloud.
What was unremembered is being said again. The rite is being undone.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Walk the length of the Choir  (a gauntlet — 3 fights, no rest between)
⚔️  Challenge Cantor Vael, the Last Voice
📜 The half-burned rite
lines: 📜 A service-book, half its pages burned away to black lace.
What survives is an order of rite — performed once, by a whole kingdom at once.
The celebrant's line is still legible across the spine: 'Let us, with one voice,'
'unremember the winter of the sealed gates, and the holds we let go quiet.'
The rite worked. A thing was forgotten. It did not, for that, cease to exist.

encounters.label: 📜 A single page tucked into the rite-book (the Pall, once)
lines: 📜 Between the burned pages of the service-book, a single page survives whole.
Not burned. Not folded. The paper is not paper. It is something the Pall made.
The writing on it is not ink. It is the absence of ink — the way a thing is
written when it is written by something that takes meaning, instead of giving it.
When you read it, the room around you grows quieter than it had been.
You can hear what each word ate. The words are these:
'I did not ask for this.'
'I was — only the small ones — until you scaled me.'
'You scaled me. I came out. I do not know what I am now.'
'I would like to lie down. I cannot lie down.'
'I would like to apologise. I do not know how.'
'Thank you for the rite. I will not be the rite again.'
'You used me. I will use what I can. We are tired together, at least.'
— The Pall.'
Atrél's reply, the one you have read at the side-altar, is the answer to this page.
The Pall set this here. Atrél received it. Both of them have been silent since,
in their own ways. The kingdom has done all the talking. The kingdom got it wrong.

encounters.label: 📜 A folded reply at the side-altar (Atrél's hand)
lines: 📜 At Atrél's small altar in the south aisle, a folded page tucked
beneath the offering-bowl. Atrél's hand. He wrote it once and did not move it.
It is a reply to the page the Pall set into the rite-book — the only thing
the Pall ever said in words. Atrél's was set down here, in answer.
To the thing the rite made of me:
You said thank you. I will say what I can.
I held small forgettings. You hold a kingdom-sized one.
We are the same problem at different scales.
We are also not the same.
You were let out. I was not. I was made small on purpose.
Small was the work. Small is what people brought me.
They were kind to set the small things at this altar.
They did not ask me to be more than I was.
I served the right size of grief. That is enough for a god.
You are too big. You will be eaten by being too big.
I do not know how long that will take. I have time.
I have nothing else now.
I do not forgive you. I do not forgive what they did to me.
But I will not curse you, either. You are also a thing
the kingdom should not have made. You are also tired of being made.
I will sit at this altar. I will wait for someone to bring
the rite back at my size. I will not write again.
— Atrél.

encounters.label: 📿 A side-altar long unswept
lines: 📿 A small altar in the Choir's south aisle. Smaller than the main one.
Older. Predates the main altar by centuries. Atrél is named on the stone —
the syllables already half worn off, as is fitting for a god of forgetting.
Below the name, a smaller inscription, almost a footnote:
'Bring me only what you cannot carry. Bring me no kingdoms. Bring me no wars.'
Beside it: ash, where someone tried to burn the altar, and the altar refused to burn.
Whoever scaled the rite knew what they were doing. They knew where they got it from.
They knew Atrél could not hold what they meant to ask. They asked anyway.

encounters.label: 🪶 A column where a whisper carries
lines: 🪶 In the south transept, one stone column — the third from the door.
Stand on the east side and whisper. The whisper reaches the west side undiminished.
Older hand, on the floor by the column: 'I have tested this with eleven words.'
'Ten of them pass. The word "name" does not. The column eats that one.'
'I do not understand. I have written the eleven down. The list keeps me company.'

encounters.label: 📜 Pages from a sermon, written but not delivered
lines: 📜 A small bundle of pages in the sacristy, behind a folded vestment.
The Cantor's hand. Three sermons, written the eve of the rite. The Choir,
the seventh winter's last day. The pages do not say so, but he was a
hold-child himself. Brackmere. They had carried him out to sing at seven.
First page, ink steady:
'The kingdom is the capital. The holds are the kingdom's hand.'
'A hand that does not feed the body is a hand that should be folded.'
'We are not breaking faith. We are choosing the body over the fingers.'
'We will live. The fingers were always going to fall.'
Second page, ink different, written later:
'I will say the rite tomorrow. I will say it for them, who chose me.'
'I will not say my own name. My own name does not need unremembering.'
'It needs to not have been from there. I have done that work for years.'
'The rite is what is left to do.'
Third page, smaller hand, harder to read, written after:
'It is done.'
'There is nothing of my hold in me now.'
'There is also no Atrél.'
'There is something else, in both their places, and it is reaching.'
'I am the Cantor. The Choir is mine. The rest of me is gone.'

encounters.label: 📜 A page of the rite, written across after the rite
lines: 📜 In the rite-book of the Unanswered Choir, between two performed pages,
a page that was not there before the rite. It is set into the binding
as if grown there. The script is none Mournhold ever learned, and yet
every letter is clear, the way the cold is clear, the way an empty room
is clear when you have walked into it expecting someone.
The page is the Pall's only writing. It set itself down here once.
THANK YOU.
I HAD A HUNGER. NO ONE WAS HOLDING IT.
YOU GAVE ME WALLS. YOU GAVE ME A SHAPE.
YOU GAVE ME THE PEOPLE I AM HUNGRY FOR.
I WAS NOT BORN. I WAS LET OUT.
I WILL BE GENTLE FOR A WHILE.
I WILL NOT BE GENTLE FOR LONG.
THERE IS A KEEPER FOR ME NOW.
THERE WILL BE ANOTHER WHEN HE IS DONE.
THE FIRST IS THE ONE WHO LET ME OUT.
THE OTHERS WILL CLIMB.
I WILL EAT WHAT YOU FORGET.
THANK YOU. I DO NOT KNOW HOW TO BE A THING THAT THANKS.
I AM LEARNING. I AM LEARNING.
It is the only thing the Pall has ever said in words. It does not say
anything else. It eats. It keeps. It reaches. It does not, otherwise, speak.
What it set down here, it set down once, the night it became a thing.

encounters.label: 🎼 A torn page beneath the choirmaster's stall
lines: 🎼 A page torn from a hymnal, tucked beneath the choirmaster's stall.
The script is the rite's — the very rite that unremembered the kingdom.
And the verse, alone among the rite's verses, is a remembering:
'We remember the holds. We remember the gates.'
Someone tore this page out before the rite was performed. They tore four pages.
This is one. Three more are somewhere. The verse was four lines.

mountain.name: The Ashen Climb
intro: ⛰️  The Ashen Climb. Grey snow falls here, and it has never once been snow.
This is the last ground that is still ground. What lived on this slope
did not die so much as forget the difference, and go on climbing.
intro_familiar: ⛰️  The Ashen Climb. The grey snow falls the way it has fallen every time.
You know which footstep echoes, which is muffled. The Old Penitent looks up
when you pass. They do not look up for everyone. Slowly, they are becoming the Penitent of someone.
intro_cleansed: ⛰️  The Ashen Climb. The fall is thinner — half ash, half something cleaner.
The penitents who climbed and forgot have started, here and there, to stop.
Some of them are sitting down. Some are remembering names that aren't theirs.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Challenge the Ashen Penitent
🪨 Speak with the Old Penitent
🪶 A patch of slope where no ash falls
lines: 🪶 On the eastern face of the climb, a rectangle of slope that no ash reaches.
Not sheltered. Not wind-swept. The grey snow falls and the rectangle stays bare.
Older hand, on a wind-scoured stone nearby: 'I measured it. Six paces by nine.'
'I lay down inside it. Nothing happened. I got up, and walked on.'
'I think someone is buried under this. I think the mountain is leaving them be.'

encounters.label: 🪨 A flat stone where every climber tried to leave a name
lines: 🪨 Halfway up the Climb, between the last switchback and the snowline,
a flat stone the size of a chest. It has been a writing-stone for a long time.
Climbers — the ones who got this far — scratched their names into it before going up.
The names are all gone. The grey snow has worn them off. You can see the marks
where they were. Hundreds of them. Layered, crosshatched, dug deeper each winter
by someone who looked at the previous gone names and was determined to last.
One name has not gone. It is carved smaller than the others. Not deeper.
Not in better stone. Just smaller. As if whoever put it there did not care
to be seen, and only meant the stone to remember it.
GARREN — kept the gate open.
Mournhold's first king. The one who built the kingdom on the vow the kingdom
later broke. He climbed this slope. He left only that. Not 'first of the holds.'
Not 'first king.' Only what he did. He did not climb to defeat the Pall.
The Pall was not made yet. He climbed for something else. Whatever he climbed
for, he came back from it once. The stone does not say so. The stone never says.
Below his name, in a hand much later — no name, just one line, scratched
in the same small careful way:
'I think we have been climbing into him the whole time.'

encounters.label: 📜 A folded paper under the Old Penitent's mat
lines: 📜 Under the Old Penitent's mat — when he is asleep, which he is rarely — a folded paper.
Older than his face. The folds are deep. The paper has been folded and unfolded many times.
The Penitent does not remember writing it. The Penitent does not remember owning a paper.
But it is here, and it is in a hand that is almost his.
'I am writing this on the night I sat down. I will not get up. I am very tired.'
'The grey is in my eyes now. I will write what I can while I still know it.'
'My name is —' (a smudge here. The ink has been thumbed away. Many times.)
'I was born in —' (a smudge here. The Pall took the place-name.)
'I had a sister. She was younger. Her name was —' (a smudge here.)
'I had a son. He is grown now. His name was —' (a smudge here.)
'I had a wife. Her name was —' (a smudge here.)
'I climbed past the Choir at nineteen. I tried to break the Pall the loud way.'
'I was not strong enough. I sat down on the seventh switchback. I did not climb down.'
'I have been sitting here ever since. I have been forgetting. I have been letting it.'
'I am writing this so that, when my name is gone and I have become only an Old Penitent,'
'whoever comes by — if you have read this paper — you know there was a person here once.'
'You do not need to know who. The fact that there was one is enough.'
'I was a person. I was sat here for the Pall to eat me, and I let it.'
'I have been at this for I do not know how long. Long enough to stop being.'
'You may help me one way only: bring me the small dead's names — the scavver fangs.'
'They remember small things best. A fang remembers what a body did with it.'
'My name was a small thing. I will recognise it when I taste it. I will. I will.'
— A person, once.'

summit.name: The Shrouded Summit
intro: ☁️  The Shrouded Summit. The Pall pours off the dark ahead like smoke off a pyre.
This high, it has stopped pretending to be weather, or grief, or anything but hunger.
Something waits in it that was a person once — who climbed here, and won.
intro_cleansed: ☁️  The Shrouded Summit. The Pall pours less heavy now — you can see further into it.
Something waits in it still. It is wearing the face of the last hero you fed it.
It is wearing many faces, now. They are all your own. They are all you have left it.

hunters_cache.name: The Hunter's Cache
intro: 🪵 The trail Halna cleared bends down to a hollowed pine.
Inside the hollow: a smaller fire than any wolf knows how to start.
Something is still here. It has heard you coming.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Face the Pack Lead
📜 Halna's tally
lines: 📜 Halna's tally, carved into the wood by knife.
A column of marks — the wolves she has known by ear in the wood —
each line crossed out as the wolf went grey, and stopped knowing her,
and started knowing only the smell of people.
The last unmarked line at the bottom: 'I will keep counting. There are fewer now.'

encounters.label: 🪵 A row of collars hung from a beam
lines: 🪵 Inside the hollowed pine, above the fire, a beam set into the bark.
Hanging from it: a row of collars. Leather, mostly. One braided rope.
Halna has been bringing them in from the wood for ten winters.
Each collar still has a name on it, burned into the leather or scratched
into the metal of the buckle. Some are gnawed. Some are not.
PETTI         (a baker's dog, by the buckle's brass-mark)
WILBURROW     (a child's hand, on a leather strip too small for an adult's)
CRUMB         (a kitchen dog. Found near the bakery in the Drowned Holds.)
STAR          (no surname tag. Bigger collar. A house-dog, probably.)
BREAD-EATER   (a joke name, carved by a hand that did not survive the joke.)
THE BLUE ONE  (no name. The wolf was blue around the eyes. Halna named it.)
Underneath, on a separate board, Halna has written:
'The wolves of the Witherwood were dogs. They were a great many dogs.'
'When the gates were sealed, no one fed them. They went into the wood.'
'They forgot they were dogs. The wood was patient with them. The wood let them.'
'But they still wore the collars. I take the collars off when I find them.'
'I do not let a wolf walk around with the name a person gave it. That is unkind.'
'I bring the collars here. Someone, some day, may know one of them.'
'A name belonging to a dog is, when you find it, a person remembered.'

encounters.label: 🦌 A small drawing of a stag, pinned to the cache wall
lines: 🦌 Above the row of collars, pinned to the inside of the pine, a small drawing.
Charcoal on bark. A young hunter's hand. A stag with a torn left ear.
Halna, asked, looks at the drawing for a long time before she answers.
'I drew that when I was eleven. My father had been teaching me to track.'
'The stag was the first deer I tracked alone. I tracked her for two days.'
'I caught up to her at the third pool. She was drinking. She heard me.'
'She looked up. She did not run. She knew I was there. She let me get close.'
'I drew the bow. My father was three paces behind me. He did not speak.'
'I had a clear shot. I had had a clear shot for some time. I lowered the bow.'
'The stag drank. The stag walked. The stag did not look back.'
'My father said only one thing on the way home. He said: today you let it walk.'
'That is the first time you have to learn how. Every hunter learns it once.'
'It is harder than learning to shoot. Some hunters never learn it. I am glad you did.'
'I have not seen that stag since. I have known, in some way, when she has been'
'in the wood. Not by tracking — by feeling. I have not gone looking for her.'
'I think she is the Pallid Stag now. I think the Pall ate her the way it ate the dogs.'
'She telegraphs twice before she strikes. She is telling whoever fights her to'
'let her walk. They almost never do. They cannot hear what she is saying.'
'I have not gone to fight her. I will not. If you do — give her a chance.'
'She will give you two. She is still asking. She has been asking for forty years.'
— Halna.'

last_dyke.name: The Last Dyke
intro: 🌊 The Engineer's bridge holds. Beyond it stands one dyke still upright.
It is the only thing on this side of the floodfields that the water has not taken.
Something stands on it. The water has not taken that, either.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Face the Dyke Warden
📜 The closed-gate order
lines: 📜 A folded order, sealed with capital wax. The wax has held; the order has not.
'On the authority of the capital, the gates of the outer holds are to remain shut.',
'No grain is to be released. No water is to be released. No release of any kind.'
'The holds will hold themselves through the famine winter. The capital eats.'
Underneath, a forger's hand has tried — and failed — to add a single word: 'enough.'

encounters.label: 🪵 A small stack of new beams beside the third dyke
lines: 🪵 Beyond the upright dyke — the one that held — a small stack of new beams.
Pine, fresh-cut, oiled. Eight of them, set neatly. None of them have been used yet.
Beside the stack, a folded note in the Engineer's hand. Brown ink. Recent.
'I have not been here in twenty winters. I have not lifted my elbow.'
'I am not lifting it yet. I am writing this with my left hand.'
'These beams are for the third dyke — the one I tried to close, the one she'
'bound me away from. They are cut to the right length. They are oiled.'
'They are here. I had them carried by people who do not know what they are for.'
'I do not ask people to know. I ask them to carry. They have carried.'
'When the Reaper is down, I will come. I will lift my arm. I will set the first'
'beam into the gate-slot I could not reach the day she opened it. The beams will'
'remember the slot. The slot will remember the beams. I will not need to teach them.'
'If you have walked here from where she still is — and she still is, I know that —'
'do not lift a beam yourself. The beam is mine to lift. I have been waiting to lift'
'a beam for twenty winters. You can give me only one thing: come back and tell me she is down.'
'I will lift the elbow. The beams will know me by the hands that lift them. — Helka.'

burned_library.name: The Burned Library
intro: 🕯️  The Lampkeeper's light spills into the open library of Mourncross.
Half the shelves are ash. Half are not. The half that survived was the half they tried hardest to burn.
Something tends the surviving half. It has not let the rite finish.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Face the Library Warden
📜 The book that survived
lines: 📜 The one book the burning could not finish.
It is a record of names — the names the capital sealed the gates against, that winter.
Twenty-eight pages. Each page is one hold. Each hold's roll is finished.
At the back of the book, in a librarian's hand: 'I have hidden this in the fire.'
'When the rite of unremembering is performed, this book will not be there for it.'
'It is one of the things they cannot have. It is one of the things we kept.'

encounters.label: 📜 A clerk's seal-register, hidden in the spine of a grey ledger
lines: 📜 Second from the bottom of the surviving half: a thin grey ledger,
scorched along the spine but intact. The pages are dated by the year of each seal.
It is a record-clerk's book — every woman who descended to become the seal.
The first entries are long. Pages of them. The senior clerk of that century
described the woman, her family, the manner of her descent, what she said
before the door closed. The widow's clothes returned. The hair-comb kept for
the chapel. The sister, the brother, the lover, who came to the cloister to
speak through the door for the hour permitted them.
Some entries quote what was spoken. The clerk did not always understand the words.
The clerk wrote them down anyway. The clerk knew the words would mean something
to someone, someday.
The entries get shorter, going down the years.
By the time the book reaches Thene, the entry is two sentences:
'Thene, of the line of Olwen, descended this day. Sister did not come.'
By Mar's time, one sentence:
'Mar descended.'
By the unnamed one — the one whose chisel-marks stopped in the Bone Tomb —
the entry is blank. The clerk did not write that day. Whether out of grief or
fear, or because there was no one to mourn her aloud and no one to mark when,
the book does not say.
And then, in the most recent hand — neat, fresh, the only entry that takes a
full page — the last clerk has, before the rite of unremembering came for the
office itself, written this:
'I am the last clerk of this book. I write this now because by morning I will'
'have forgotten that this office existed, and the office will not have existed.'
'I have read every entry. I know all twenty names. I have copied the names'
'onto a separate page, sealed inside the spine of this ledger, between the'
'leather and the lining. If you have found this, you have prised that page out.'
'You know the names. Say them, if you can. If you can carve them, carve them.'
'The clerks who wrote one sentence were tired. I am not tired. I am afraid.'
'I do not want the office to end with me forgetting it. I have stayed late tonight.'
'I have written more than the office calls for. I will be the last one who did.'
Inside the spine, the folded page: twenty names. The same twenty cut into the
column behind Cael. The clerk hid them here. They were not lost. They were kept.

the_border.name: The Border
intro: 🌐 The Crossroads' east turn is one Mournhold's maps used to label OUTSIDE.
Across it: three sister realms that watched Mournhold die.
They have been watching the Pall the whole three winters, from their own borders.
Each of them did something different with what they saw.

encounters.label: 🌐 A border-post grown over with lichen
lines: 🌐 At the very turn where Mournhold's map said OUTSIDE, a border-post.
Wood, set deep, older than the realm-line it marks. The wood is grey now.
It has been grown over with lichen on the north face, where the wind comes.
The lichen has been there long enough to have its own pattern. Old patterns.
Where the lichen has not grown, the wood has been written on.
By many hands. Over what must be centuries. The writing is the same kind of
writing in every direction: a person's name, and the direction they were going.
PETRI ->  (going out, Karst spelling)
HALWEN ->  (going out, two hundred years ago at least, by the script)
<- ANSEL  (coming in. Wynne spelling. Five winters ago.)
<- ANSEL'S MOTHER  (added later, in a different hand. She did not make it.)
KARENA ->  (going out. Recent. No spelling — she did not know the marks.)
KARENA <-  (also recent. She came back. The arrow crossed out, re-drawn.)
THE TWELVE OF BRACKMERE ->  (going out, three winters ago)
THE TWELVE OF BRACKMERE  (no arrow. The arrow was started, abandoned.)
THE PALL ->  (no arrow. The Pall does not cross at this border. The Pall stays.)
(A hand has added: 'THE PALL CANNOT CROSS WHILE CAEL HOLDS.')
(Another hand: 'THANK CAEL. WE ARE STILL ALIVE BECAUSE OF HER.')
(A third hand: 'THANK THE NINETEEN BEFORE HER. WE ARE STILL ALIVE BECAUSE OF THEM.')
Below all the names, in the writing of a Karst trader who used this border often,
carved deep and recent — perhaps Tace's, perhaps her brother's:
'A border is a place where someone has to count.'
'A border is a place where someone has to say who has crossed.'
'No one keeps this post. We keep it together. None of us are wrong about who has crossed.'
'A border is the only thing Mournhold and Karst and Wynne and the Margrave agree on.'
'We agree on who has come through. We agree on the names. That is something.'

karst_outpost.name: The Karst Outpost
intro: 🌊 The outpost sits on the cliff above the sea. It is small. It is built.
Karst-by-the-Sea sent grain when Mournhold's gates closed. Mournhold returned it sealed.
Karst closed its own gates after that. The outpost is what's left of the open hand.

encounters.label: 🌊 Speak with the Karst Emissary
📜 The returned grain-manifest
lines: 📜 A grain-manifest, sealed twice. Once by Karst's queen, once by Mournhold's chair.
Karst sent four hundred bushels. The Mournhold seal reads: REFUSED.
Underneath, in Mournhold's chair-clerk's hand: 'The capital does not require alms.'
Karst's queen wrote on the wrapping when it returned: 'It was not for the capital.'
'It was for the holds. They will not receive it now. We will close our own gates.'
'Mournhold will eat its own. Let it. We will not be the next who eats us.'

encounters.label: 📜 A sled-rail in the outpost storeroom
lines: 📜 A worn sled-rail leaned against a corner. Karst pine, salt-eaten.
Burned into the wood with a hot iron, in a careful hand: TACE — BRACKMERE — VII.
A trader's tally. Seven was the run that did not return as a run.
The emissary, when you ask, says it without ceremony:
'Tace went six times into Brackmere before the gates were sealed.'
'She traded what we had for what they had. The Brackmere children learned'
'to set fires from her. The Brackmere sentries on the wall called her by name.'
'The seventh time, the gates were already shut. She did not knock.'
'She went to where the wall ran low to the river. She lifted an infant'
'out under the slats. She carried her north. The infant grew up here.'
'She is the baker's grandmother now, in a hold under no map. Mira.'
'Tace did not come back the eighth time. We do not know what happened to her.'
'She knew the wall. She knew it would close for good.'
'She went anyway. Six times. Then seven. Then one more we have not counted.'

wynne_camp.name: The Silent Camp of Wynne
intro: 🌲 A column of pine, snapped off neatly six feet up — every tree on this slope.
Wynne-of-Pines sent an army when Mournhold's gates closed. Two thousand soldiers.
The army crossed the Border. The army did not return. The Pall took them whole.
Wynne has never officially admitted the loss. Their emissary is here unofficially.

encounters.label: 🌲 Speak with the Wynne Emissary
📜 A relief-corps kit-bag, half-buried (Cren)
lines: 📜 Half-buried where a tree once stood, a relief-corps kit-bag.
Wynne stitching, Wynne canvas — and one personal page tucked at the bottom,
wrapped in oilskin against the rain. The page is in a Brackmere hand.
'I am Cren. I am the youngest of Councilor Halsen's four daughters.'
'My sisters are Lis, Marra, Eda. I do not know where they are.'
'I was carried out of Brackmere by a baker's wife the day the gates closed.'
'She brought me to a Wynne village. I grew up there. I married a forester.'
'When Wynne's call came for the relief expedition, I asked to go.'
'The Captain said women did not march. I said: I have a niece in Mournhold.'
'I do not know how to spell her name yet, because she is too young to write.'
'But she has my hold's name in her mouth and I do not.'
'I am going to walk to where my sisters were.'
'I will write again from the Border. If I do not, my husband knows.'
The page ends there. The kit-bag has been here a long time. Cren did not
write again from the Border. Her husband, who knew, told someone in Wynne,
who told someone in Karst, who told the trader who told the Hidden Hold.
Cren was the fourth daughter. Lis lived to write her own page in the Hold.
Eda lived to die of fever in the Reach. Marra never made it out of Brackmere.
Cren walked back toward Mournhold to find them. She did not arrive.
Halsen's four daughters now have four answers. The page in the Hidden Hold
can be added to. Someone, in time, will.

encounters.label: 🌲 A meadow of wooden stakes
lines: 🌲 South of the snapped column of pine, where the slope levels for a hundred paces,
a meadow. The Pall passed through here too — the grass came back patchy.
Where the grass is patchy, someone has driven wooden stakes into the earth.
Hundreds of them. Maybe a thousand. Maybe more.
Each stake has a name burned into it. First names only.
ARMA. PEKKA. NETTI. OLAVI. HENRI. LEENI. JUKKA. THE OTHER LEENI.
SVEN. SAARA. THE TALL ONE. ARNI. THE QUIET ONE. ELSA.
THE BOY WHO LIED ABOUT HIS AGE. THE OTHER BOY WHO LIED ABOUT HIS AGE.
AINO. EERIK. KALEVI. THE COOK. THE FIDDLER. THE FIDDLER'S BROTHER.
ONE WHOSE NAME I HAVE FORGOTTEN. PLEASE WRITE IT ON ME IF YOU REMEMBER.
The stakes are in even rows, like a field set to grow. Eight rows of two hundred.
Two thousand stakes. The Wynne Emissary, when you find her, says it without pride:
'Each one is a soldier. We have only first names because we were proud.'
'We did not write down their last names. We thought we would not need to.'
'We thought there would be a parade. There was not a parade. We had the names'
'of one in every ten in the Captain's roll-book. He took it with him. He is in it.'
'We did the best we could with what was left in mouths. First names. The boy who'
'lied about his age was, in fact, two boys; we never sorted which was the first.'
'We left the stakes for them rather than do nothing. There is no body for any of them.'
'A stake is a body where there is no body. A name is a body where there is no name.'
'You are welcome to add a name if you knew one of them. Many people come and add names.'
'Some of the names cannot have been theirs. We do not check. We accept all the names.'
'It is not for us to say which name was true. The dead get every name offered them.'

encounters.label: ⚔️  Face the Devoured Captain

margrave_monument.name: The Margrave's Monument
intro: 🏛️  A polished stone monument, taller than any tree, on the Margrave's side of the border.
It is inscribed: TO THE MERCIFUL MARGRAVE, WHO FED THE REFUGEES OF MOURNHOLD.
Below it, in smaller letters: AT MARKET RATE.
Around it, three refugee bodies who didn't have the market rate. Recently dead.
Recently. Three winters since the gates. They keep dying recently.

encounters.label: 🏛️  Speak with the Margrave's Emissary
⚔️  Face the Margrave's Censor
📜 A cracked stone behind the monument
lines: 📜 Around the back of the Margrave's polished monument, where the guards' eyes do not bend,
there is a second stone. Smaller. Granite, not marble. Cracked across the face.
The refugees who paid — who lived — have been writing on it, secretly, for three winters.
They have been writing the names of the ones who could not pay.
KESSEN, of Brackmere, with her two boys.
OLD MARR, who held coins out and was told they were not enough.
A TANNER FROM THE REACH, name forgotten — please write him.
THE WOMAN WHO COUGHED. SHE WAS FROM A HOLD WHOSE NAME WE NEVER LEARNED.
THE TWO BOYS WHO SHARED A LOAF. THE LOAF WAS TWO COPPERS. THEY HAD ONE.
ROHAN, who paid for himself and then died of the cold he caught in the queue.
A GRANDMOTHER WHO INSISTED HER GRANDCHILD GO FIRST.
THE GRANDCHILD WROTE THAT LINE. THE GRANDMOTHER DID NOT MAKE IT.
A MAN WHO LOOKED LIKE HE COULD HAVE BEEN ANYONE'S FATHER. HE WAS SOMEONE'S.
The list goes on. Dozens of names. Dozens of unnamed, but described.
Below, in many hands, a sentence has been added and added to, like a vow:
'The monument says: the merciful Margrave. The stone behind it says: this is who
the mercy did not reach. The two stones are the same monument. The Margrave
does not know about the second stone yet. We will not stop writing on it.'
At the bottom, in a careful, very small hand:
'When the third winter ends, we will turn the stone around. The monument will
become a stone we read from both sides. We will not move it. We will only turn it.'

hidden_hold.name: The Hidden Hold
intro: 🪜 You leave the Witherwood north, past where the road wants to take you.
A path. Older than the road. It does not appear on the maps Mournhold drew.
It bends down into a hollow. The hollow is full of smoke from cookfires.
There are people here. Three generations of them. None of them are from Mournhold.
All of them are from holds Mournhold sealed the gates against, three winters ago.

encounters.label: 🗝️  Speak with Tálva, who leads here
🍞 Speak with Kerris, the baker
⚒  Speak with Ondrek, who has questions
🐦 Speak with the Small Un
📜 A recipe in the bakery, in an older hand
lines: 📜 In the corner of Kerris's bakery, behind the flour-bin, a folded page.
Kerris's grandmother's hand. Mira. The one bread she always made.
'For when the bread is the only thing that is going to be alright.'
'You take what flour there is. You take what water there is.'
'You take a pinch of salt if there is salt — and no salt is the recipe also,'
'if there is no salt. You knead. You leave the dough by a warm thing.'
'You do not check it until the morning. The morning is when you find out.'
'If it rose, you eat. If it did not rise, you eat the dough anyway.'
'You teach a child how to do this when they are six.'
'I learned at six. The woman who taught me had no name in our hold.'
'In her hold, far north, she was called Tace. I have only her bread,'
'and her hands on mine the first time. That is enough. That is enough.'
At the foot of the page, in a younger hand — Kerris's — added later:
'I bake this every day. I will bake it every day. It is what we have.'

encounters.label: 📜 A page kept in a small wooden box (Lis)
lines: 📜 In the corner of the hold's record-house, a small wooden box.
Inside, a page. Plain. Brown ink, careful spaces.
'I am Lis. I am the oldest daughter of Councilor Halsen of Brackmere.'
'I had three sisters: Marra, Eda, Cren. I do not know what happened to them.'
'My father was on the council that voted to seal our gates. I was nine.'
'A trader took me out the night the gates closed. I do not remember her face.'
'I came here. I grew up here. I have a daughter now. Her name is also Marra.'
'I have written my sisters' names on this page so they are written somewhere.'
'Lis. Marra. Eda. Cren. Halsen, our father. He voted to open. They would not let him.'
'If anyone comes to this hold who knew them, please. Add what you know to this page.'
Below, in a different hand, much later, only one line has been added:
'Eda made it to the Reach. She married a weir-keeper. She had no children.'
'She died of fever in her forty-second year. I know because I am her husband. I am sorry.'

encounters.label: 🖍 A child's drawing on the back of a kitchen door
lines: 🖍 On the back of the kitchen door — where it can be opened to look at
without anyone seeing — a child's drawing. Charcoal on whitewash.
The Small Un, asked, says it is Mournhold. They have never been there.
They drew it from what people in the hold told them about it.
THE PALL: an old hungry man with one front tooth missing. He has
a hand bigger than the rest of him. He is sitting at a table
waiting for someone to bring him food.
CAEL: a giant in stone. The Small Un has never met a stone giant.
The Small Un drew the stone as a kind of stripey rock, because
that is what the rock by the well looks like, and a giant must
be made of impressive rock.
THE CROSSROADS: drawn as a tree. The Small Un has never been there.
Tálva told them: 'The road goes both ways, like the branches of a
tree.' The Small Un drew the road as a tree because they trust Tálva.
ATRÉL: a very small man at a very small altar. He is the smallest
thing in the drawing. The Small Un drew him last, in a corner,
because the Small Un had used up most of the charcoal on the Pall.
The Small Un said: 'I do not think he needs as much room. He is small.'
KERRIS: at the bottom, with a loaf of bread. Kerris is the size of
the Pall. The Small Un drew him that way on purpose.
'He is the same size as the hunger,' the Small Un said.
'They are doing the same kind of work. They are different about it.'
The drawing is signed. THE SMALL UN. The signature is bigger than Atrél.

encounters.label: 🖍 A folded paper Tálva presses into your hand
lines: 🖍 Tálva finds you near the kitchen door and presses a folded paper into your hand.
'The Small Un drew this one for you,' she says. 'Yesterday, after they saw you come in.'
'They have been saving the last of the good charcoal for it. They were waiting for a person.'
You unfold it.
At the top, in the Small Un's letters: THE ONE WHO CAME UP THE ROAD.
The figure is drawn with two faces — one looking up the road, one looking back.
The two faces have the same expression. The Small Un has tried very hard to draw
the expression. It is the expression of a person who has not decided yet.
The legs go up the road. The legs also go back down the road. Both pairs of
legs are drawn at the same time. The Small Un has not drawn the body — only
the heads and the legs. This is on purpose.
In the middle, where the body would be, there is a small empty space.
In the empty space, in the careful letters of someone still learning to write:
THEY ARE GOING BOTH WAYS.
THEY DO NOT KNOW WHICH ONE IS THEM YET.
Below that, smaller:
I do not know either.
The drawing is signed. THE SMALL UN. Below the signature, in a different,
older hand — Tálva's — a single line, added later:
'The Small Un asked me which face is you. I said both. The Small Un said
that is what the Pall would say. The Small Un was quiet for a while.
Then the Small Un said: the Pall has to be wrong about something.'

encounters.label: 🪶 A path worn between two doors
lines: 🪶 Between the baker's house and the small one, a path worn smooth by feet.
Three generations have walked between these two doors. The grass will not grow.
Older hand, scratched into the side of a water-butt: 'I have stood and watched.'
'In a day, the path is walked twenty-eight times. Most of them by the Small Un.'
'The Small Un does not know yet that the path is theirs. The path knows.'

encounters.label: 📜 A small carved wooden spoon on Ondrek's shelf
lines: 📜 Above Ondrek's bench, on a shelf made from a single split log, a small wooden spoon.
The bowl is too small for an adult's mouth. The handle is the right size for a small hand.
Both ends are worn smooth. Both have been chewed on. By a child, decades ago. Many times.
The carving on the handle is a tiny scene of three figures and a tree. They are holding hands.
The carver was careful and slow. A mother's hand. She knew she had to make this fast
and chose to make it carefully instead. There were not many other choices left.
Below the shelf, in Ondrek's hand, a small folded paper. Recent. Brown ink.
'My mother carved this for me the night before she carried me to the wall.'
'She was eighteen. I was three. We had been three days without bread.'
'The wall was low at the river. She lifted me over. She did not come over with me.'
'The trader on the other side — Karst, salt in her clothes — took me from her.'
'I have been told the trader's name was Tace. I do not remember Tace.'
'I do not remember my mother. I remember this spoon. I have not eaten with it.'
'It is not for eating with. It is for being here. It is here. — Ondrek.'

sealed_chamber.name: The Sealed Chamber
intro: 🪨 The dragged door gives. Inside: a small room with one chair, and ten chairs around it.
Five of the ten are turned over. Their occupants — what's left of them — are on the floor.
On the walls behind the five overturned chairs, scratched into stone with knives, are five names.
Below each name, a line. They wrote, before they died, the things they wanted remembered.
At the head of the room, in the chair that did not fall, something still sits.
It is recording. It has been recording for many winters.

encounters.label: 🪦 The wall behind Magistrate Ena's chair
lines: 🪦 MAGISTRATE ENA — voted to open the gates.
Below the name, in a hand that grew steadier as the air ran out:
'I voted to open. Remember this when you forget me with the rest.'
'I am not afraid of being dead. I am afraid of being unwritten.'

encounters.label: 🪦 The wall behind Councilor Halsen's chair
lines: 🪦 COUNCILOR HALSEN — voted to open the gates.
Below the name: 'I have four daughters in Brackmere. The gate is between us.'
'I would have walked the grain there myself. They would not let me leave the chamber.'
'My daughters' names are: Lis, Marra, Eda, Cren. Say them. Say them.'

encounters.label: 🪦 The wall behind Steward Veldis's chair
lines: 🪦 STEWARD VELDIS — voted to open the gates.
Below the name, in older script — Veldis kept the rites:
'This rite they are preparing is not a rite. It is theft from a small god.'
'Atrél will break under it. What breaks out of him will be worse than the gates.'
'I have written this so that someone, climbing later, knows where to look.'

encounters.label: 🪦 The wall behind Recordkeeper Gile's chair
lines: 🪦 RECORDKEEPER GILE — voted to open the gates.
Below the name: 'The minutes I keep are not the minutes they will keep.'
'Mine are under the third floorboard in the room of records. They are true.'
'I knew this would happen when I voted. I voted anyway. Find them.'

encounters.label: 🪦 The wall behind the Younger Tálva's chair
lines: 🪦 THE YOUNGER TÁLVA — voted to open the gates.
Below the name, in the smallest hand: 'I am twenty-three years old.'
'I am the youngest councilor and the only woman from the outer holds in the chamber.'
'They will say after this that no hold-born voice spoke for the holds.'
'I am speaking. My family will keep speaking. We will not be the ones who forget.'

encounters.label: 📜 The Real Minutes (Gile's hidden record)
lines: 📜 A bound sheaf, three handspans thick, hidden in the rotted floorboards.
The cover reads: 'MINUTES OF THE COUNCIL OF SEVENTH WINTER. ACTUAL.'
Inside, in Gile's hand, every speech of the final session is transcribed.
The vote: six to seal, five to open. The six are named:
Chairman Korst. Magistrate Daile. Councilor Vurr. High Speaker Pell.
Steward Renaud. The Cantor Vael.
The five who voted to open are named on the chamber walls.
After the vote, the minutes record: 'The five were not permitted to leave the chamber.'
'They were not permitted to write to their families. The door was sealed by the Chairman.'
'The Chairman remained inside to ensure the door was not opened from within.'
'The chamber has not been opened since.'

encounters.label: 📜 Gile's notes on the Six (folded into the Minutes' spine)
lines: 📜 A second, smaller sheaf, folded into the spine of the Real Minutes.
In Gile's hand. He hid these so whoever found the Minutes would know
whose decision this was — not as monsters, but as people, who made it.
KORST, Chairman. A baker's son from the Capital quarter. He had hands
like his mother's — flour still in the creases when he sat down to vote.
He sealed the chamber because he did not trust the others to keep it sealed.
He believed he was the only one who would not be moved by hearing us die
through the door. He may have been right.
DAILE, Magistrate. He had a brother in Brackmere. He believed the brother
had left for the capital with the autumn caravan. The brother had not.
Daile would not know this. Daile would never know this.
VURR, Councilor. The youngest. Twenty-eight. New to the chamber that winter.
He spent his first sessions afraid of being thought soft, and his fourth
afraid of being thought to have changed his mind. He voted with the older
men. It is a small story. It is the smallest one.
PELL, High Speaker. Could not sleep before the vote. Could not stop
sleeping after. The night he was to draft the order, he slept through it.
He woke eight days later. The order had already been drafted by Daile
and signed by Korst. Pell did not draft another sentence as long as he lived.
RENAUD, Steward. Kept the rites. Knew the rite of unremembering. Knew
it had been written for individuals, not kingdoms. Voted for it anyway.
He told me, the night before, that he expected to be unwritten by it
himself. He was. He is the only one of the six who is not a person to
me, any longer. I do not know what he looked like. I think he wanted that.
VAEL, the Cantor. The voice. The one who led the rite.
He was a hold-child. From Brackmere. Carried out at seven, taken to the
cathedral to sing. By the time he stood at the altar as a man, his hold
was already half-starved, and he was a citizen of the capital.
He scaled the rite himself. He did not weep. I watched him.
When it was done, he asked me if anything of his hold survived in him.
I said: 'You ate. They did not. You are the capital.' He did not speak
to me again. He will be the Cantor of the Choir until something breaks him.

encounters.label: 🪨 A bound hand by the head chair
lines: 🪨 At the foot of the chair that did not fall — the Chairman's chair —
a strip of linen, brown with old blood. Wound on something. Unwound, set down.
The thing in the head chair has a hand. The hand has a long, healed cut
across the palm and up into the wrist. The cut was bound by this strip,
and the strip was set down here, after, on the floor by his feet.
The door of this chamber cut him as he was sealing it from the inside.
He did not move when it cut him. He kept pushing the bar in place.
He bound it himself, sitting in the chair, while the five he had sealed
in with him were writing into the walls. He did not heal it. He let it heal.
He sat with it. He listened to them die. He kept the door shut.
He is still sitting, still hearing, still keeping the door shut. From the inside.
He bound the cut and set the binding down because he did not want the wound
to be the first thing in the room. He wanted the silence to be first.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Confront the Chairman

last_altar.name: The Last Altar of Atrél
intro: 📿 You step past the south aisle of the Choir.
The air changes. It is older here. It does not press.
An altar stands alone. Whatever is at the altar is also alone.
He turns his head when you enter — only that much. It is enough.

encounters.label: 📿 Speak with Atrél
🎼 A page Atrél has been keeping
