lines: 🎼 Atrél, without speaking, places a folded page on the altar between you.
It is older than the page you found in the Choir. Same rite. Same script.
But the words are in Atrél's hand — copied here for safekeeping, long ago.
'We remember the names. We remember the rain.'
Beneath the line, in the smallest writing: 'I have kept this for whoever came back.'
'A god of forgetting cannot sing a remembering. Only one of you can. Find the others.'

encounters.label: 📜 A bound folio Atrél has been keeping under the altar
lines: 📜 Atrél, without speaking, lifts the cloth from the altar's base.
Underneath, a small bound folio. Older than the rite-book in the Choir.
He sets it on the altar between you and does not open it.
You open it. The hand inside is not Atrél's. It is older. Verren's, maybe.
The first page is a title, in the old script that survived the rite:
THE RITE OF UNREMEMBERING.
For a person, for one loss, when remembering will kill them.
Below the title, in a smaller hand — Atrél's, much later — a single note:
'This was a kindness. We did kindnesses. Read what kindness was.'
You read. The rite is ten lines. It is for one person, kneeling at
Atrél's altar, with one thing they cannot bear. The priest names what
the person names. Atrél holds it. The person walks out into the world
without that one thing — light by exactly the weight of that one loss.
The rite cannot take more. The rite was made small on purpose. A god of
forgetting needs to be small enough to carry the forgetting and not be eaten by it.
On the last page, in Atrél's hand, written shakily, after the kingdom's rite:
'Renaud, the Steward, took this folio for a winter. He returned it.'
'He had read it. He scaled it anyway. He used the words. He kept the structure.'
'He did not keep the rule that says: one person, one loss. He took that page out.'
'I have written it back in. I have written it back in three times.'
'The wrong that was done was the scale. The rite was a kindness. Renaud knew that.'
'Renaud chose to break a kindness. That is the harder thing to forgive.'
— Atrél.

pre_pall_shrine.name: The Pre-Pall Shrine
intro: 🪨 Behind the Old Penitent, the rock opens onto a low room.
An altar with no Pall-marks on it. A flame the colour flame used to be.
Something stands at the altar, hands folded, waiting to be remembered.

encounters.label: ⚔️  Face the Shrine Keeper
📜 The first vow
lines: 📜 An iron tablet. The marks on it are older than the rite of unremembering.
'We are the people of Mournhold. We will not forget our dead by name.',
'We will not seal our gates against our own. We will not let our holds go quiet.',
'We will eat last, when we eat at all, and we will say the names of the dead.'
Below: a name. The kingdom's first king. He kept the vow. Those who came after did not.

encounters.label: 📜 A fragment in older script
lines: 📜 A small inscription on the wall behind the altar, in script older than the tablet.
Only four words survive intact:
VERREN.   WE WERE.
Mournhold's archives know no Verren. No kingdom by that name has ever existed.
The script is older than the language Mournhold was written in.
Whatever Verren was, it was here first. And it ended.

encounters.label: 🪨 A sword's pommel set into the altar
lines: 🪨 Set into the front of the altar — flush with the stone — a small sword's pommel.
Brass. Worn smooth by hands. Older even than the iron tablet that names the vow.
Around the pommel, in the same older script that survived the rite,
five words are carved. The first ring of the kingdom's first king's name.
GARREN   FIRST   OF   THE   HOLDS
Not 'first of Mournhold.' Not 'first of the kingdom.' First of the holds.
He was a hold-keeper before he was a king. He named himself for what he was.
He never let his line forget that — not while he kept the vow, anyway.
After him it was forgotten. The kingdom called him only 'the first king'.
His daughter Cael, who became the seal, still says his name. Quietly. Quietly.

encounters.label: 📜 A counting-song below the fragment, in the same older hand
lines: 📜 Below the VERREN inscription, almost lost in the moss, five lines.
Smaller. Carved by the same hand. Cael's father — Mournhold's first king —
must have set them down here on the night before she descended.
It is a counting-song. A child's rhyme. The only piece of Verren that survived.
He must have learned it as a boy. He must have known he was about to forget it.
'One: the door is open.'
'Two: the bread is shared.'
'Three: the river is named.'
'Four: the dead are said.'
'Five: the door is opened again.'
The five duties Verren taught its children. Mournhold inherited every one,
and broke every one, in order, in the seventh winter. The song does not say so.
The song is for children. It was for closing doors gently at the end of a day,
and opening them again in the morning. That is what it has always been for.

encounters.label: 🎼 A line cut into the altar's underside
lines: 🎼 You kneel beneath the altar of the Pre-Pall Shrine and look up.
On the underside of the stone, where only the kneeling see, a line is cut.
The script is older than the tablet, older than the kingdom, older than the rite.
'We remember the bread we did not give.'
Someone carved this here knowing the rite would come — and knowing the kneelers would find it.
Whoever it was, they had a long view, and a heavy one.

bone_tomb.name: The Bone Tomb
intro: 🪨 The altar in the Pre-Pall Shrine is also a door. You see it now.
Beneath it: a stair, and at the foot of the stair, a small room.
A figure sits cross-legged at the centre. Ossified. The stone of the room
has grown into her, and out of her, and through her open mouth.
She is the floor. She is the walls. She is the seal.
She is alive. She turns her head only a little. She sees you.

encounters.label: ▒  Speak with Cael
🎼 A line Cael's mouth still shapes
lines: 🎼 Cael's open mouth still shapes a word, even as the stone grows through it.
You lean close. You do not look at the stone. You look at the word.
It is the last line. The line they cut out of her so she would not finish the verse.
'We remember. We remember. We remember.'
She has been holding the line in her open mouth for as long as she has been the seal.
She did not let them un-remember it. Now you have it. Now it can be sung.

encounters.label: 🪨 The column behind Cael, where the names go down
lines: 🪨 Behind where Cael sits, the wall is not wall.
It is a column of older stone, growing downward into the bedrock.
Names have been cut into it — one above the next, going down.
The top name is fresh and only half-cut. Cael's own. She is still writing it.
CAEL      daughter of Garren
KESS      daughter of Aral
THENE     daughter of Olwen
MAR       daughter of Ven
UNNAMED   [unfinished — the chisel-marks stop]
IRIA      [in an older script]
HALD      [older still]
...       [twelve more, each older, the script changing]
NO-NAME, who was the first.
Each name carved by the next seal, after the previous seal was done.
The chisel is set in a small niche at the wall's foot. It has been used many times.
It has been sharpened and re-sharpened. The handle is worn smooth by women's hands.
At the base of the column, in the oldest script — the one that survived the rite,
the one Verren used — a single line is written:
'The seal is older than the kingdom. The kingdom is the latest of many things',
'that ended with someone sitting in a small dark room and holding what was left.'
'It is not the Pall that the seal holds back. It is the unmaking of every kingdom.'
'You will not be the last to sit here. Someone, in time, will carve your name above mine.'
The line is unsigned. It must have been carved by the first one.
There is no name above it. There is only Cael, twenty names up, still writing hers.
When Cael, without speaking, sees that you have read down the column, she nods once.
The stone where her chin rests grinds against itself. Her eyes do not leave yours.
It is the look of a woman handing you something she has been holding for a long time.
